content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Opinion Request Meeting Participants          Date : Oct. 13, 1978


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Dwight

Subject:

       X serves as a Commissioner for the San Francisco
Opera House Commission. X has a 25% interest in the
Fly-By-Night Travel Service. Within the past few months
the Big-Top Ballet Company has purchased $1,000 worth of
airline tickets from X's Service. The Travel Service
has received a 5% commission on the sale of these
tickets from the affected airlines. Has X received
$250 from the Ballet Company because of her pro rata
share of airline ticket fees paid by the Ballet Company
to the Travel Service for the purposes of disqualification


NOTE: It was decided at the opinion request meeting that X
      did not have a $250.00 pro rata share. X's income was
      a pro rata share of the 5% commission on the sale of
      the airline ticket. This position had been previously
      taken by staff with respect to an earlier matter.

      This question came from the San Francisco City Attorney's
      office by telephone, and an oral response was given by
      me reflecting the decision made at the opinion request
      meeting.

                                        Dwight Dickerson

sections.question: Has X received $250 from the Ballet Company because of her pro rata share of airline ticket fees paid by the Ballet Company to the Travel Service for the purposes of disqualification?
sections.conclusion: X did not have a $250.00 pro rata share. X's income was a pro rata share of the 5% commission on the sale of the airline ticket.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a San Francisco Opera House Commissioner with a 25% interest in a travel service received disqualifying income from a ballet company's purchase of airline tickets through that service.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC determined that X did not receive disqualifying income because her pro rata share consisted only of the travel service's 5% commission on ticket sales, not a direct payment from the ballet company.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is internal FPPC memo documenting oral response to San Francisco City Attorney's office; no formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION section headers but substantive question and conclusion are clearly stated in body; question format paraphrased for clarity; oral response given before memo creation

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Has X received $250 from the Ballet Company because of her pro rata share of airline ticket fees paid by the Ballet Company to the Travel Service for the purposes of disqualification?

CONCLUSION: X did not have a $250.00 pro rata share. X's income was a pro rata share of the 5% commission on the sale of the airline ticket.

X has a 25% interest in the Fly-By-Night Travel Service. Within the past few months the Big-Top Ballet Company has purchased $1,000 worth of airline tickets from X's Service. The Travel Service has received a 5% commission on the sale of these tickets from the affected airlines. Has X received $250 from the Ballet Company because of her pro rata share of airline ticket fees paid by the Ballet Company to the Travel Service for the purposes of disqualification NOTE: It was decided at the opinion request meeting that X did not have a $250.00 pro rata share. X's income was a pro rata share of the 5% commission on the sale of the airline ticket. This position had been previously taken by staff with respect to an earlier matter. This question came from the San Francisco City Attorney's office by telephone, and an oral response was given by me reflecting the decision made at the opinion request meeting. Dwight Dickerson
embedding.summary: FPPC internal memorandum addressing whether a San Francisco Opera House Commissioner's 25% interest in a travel service resulted in disqualifying income when a ballet company purchased airline tickets through that service, concluding that income was limited to the pro rata share of commissions rather than direct ballet company payments.
